Q: Reviewed prior mocks and problem sets to identify execution gaps before later attempts.
Used GMAT Club tests for hard practice and tracked hard-question accuracy above 80%.
Stopped placing Quant first after realizing that even a strong section could drain energy and hurt later sections.
Settled on DI-Q-break-V as the best order for the author's mental energy curve.
V: Used Master Comprehension and pre-thinking to repair flawed CR and RC approaches.
Improved hard Verbal accuracy after switching from broad practice to structured Verbal methods.
Placed Verbal after the break in the final section order so it came after a reset rather than after a draining Quant-first start.
DI: Bought the DI Review book and focused on tough question types such as TPA, Table Analysis, and MSR.
Identified DI underperformance as a temperament and test-taking issue, not merely a content gap.
Moved DI to the first section so it could be attempted with the freshest mind.
Prepared for travel and energy drain with hydration, caffeine, and food planning before the final attempt.
overall: Moved from 575 to 695 across multiple attempts after early unstructured practice plateaued.
Used error logs as a central tool and revised them repeatedly before later attempts.
Tested and changed section order after official underperformance despite strong mock scores.
Used mentor accountability and structured tracking to break through plateaus.
Used AI tools to analyze mistakes, questions, and strategy when helpful.

Q: Moved away from random Quant practice and used a structured TTP plan to identify weak areas.
Had to be selective with course work while working full time, focusing on the areas most likely to move the score.
Accepted that Quant remained the comparatively weaker score but improved enough when paired with stronger V and DI.
V: Did not ignore Verbal even though it was already relatively strong.
Used continued TTP work to push Verbal higher and ultimately offset a weaker Quant result.
Treated every extra point in a strong section as valuable for the total score.
DI: Did not ignore DI just because it was initially decent.
Used structured practice and analytics to keep DI improving alongside Quant and Verbal.
overall: Moved from 555 to 685 across four attempts after replacing unorganized practice with a real plan.
Used TTP's flexible plan selectively because the full course was not realistic with full-time work.
Focused on organizing study, identifying weak areas early, and improving approach rather than grinding random questions.
Found that improvement did not require perfection or a rigid completion of every resource.

Q: Rebuilt school-day Quant concepts with high-quality question banks and detailed solutions.
Practiced more than 800 Quant questions through cementing and Scholaranium quizzes.
Used e-GMAT, GMAT Club, official guides, and mba.com mocks to refine Quant process skills.
Focused on accuracy in early official questions rather than rushing.
V: Used a structured approach rather than instinct to handle hard Verbal questions.
Applied CR pre-thinking, especially to boldface questions, by categorizing statements and their relationships.
Practiced RC daily to improve speed, structure awareness, author tone, main-point, and inference questions.
Used GMAT Club discussions and official guide passages as part of RC practice.
Reduced RC time while raising hard-passage accuracy.
DI: Developed an MSR strategy of quickly skimming all sources first to understand available information.
Answered MSR questions by returning to sources as needed and tracking connections between pieces of data.
Focused on DI time management to avoid getting bogged down in complex data.
overall: Moved from self-study that exposed gaps to a structured course approach after earlier attempts.
Transitioned from classic GMAT prep to Focus Edition prep.
Took three mba.com mocks in the final week; disappointing scores lowered pressure by making a retake feel acceptable.
Stayed calm on test day, focused on accuracy, and avoided rushing the first few questions.

Q: Followed TTP's roadmap without skipping sections or shortcuts.
Built mastery through end-of-chapter tests and practice problems organized by difficulty.
Relied on TTP explanations and problem banks rather than official guide material.
Used official mocks after completing theory to validate timing and readiness.
V: Used the same single-resource TTP approach for Verbal rather than mixing materials.
Trusted structured theory and practice to make every question type feel familiar on test day.
DI: Used TTP as the sole DI learning and practice source.
Practiced all mocks under timed conditions so DI and other sections felt familiar on test day.
overall: Studied 25-30 hours per week for about six months, treating GMAT prep like a part-time job.
Completed the full theory portion in about four months and spent the final 1.5 months on mocks and review.
Bought all six official mocks and used them under real conditions without pausing.
Outscored every mock on test day by arriving rested, calm, and focused on execution.
Emphasized consistency, timing, and long correct-answer streaks.

Q: Started from a 525 diagnostic and recognized that untimed ability existed but timed execution was slow.
Used TTP schedule mode for the first month, with roughly two Quant lessons for every Verbal lesson.
After early mocks, shifted the schedule to focus more heavily on Quant.
Added pacing practice, daily flashcard review for forgotten concepts, and more careful question reading to reduce careless mistakes.
In the final week, accelerated Quant coverage by doing one easy, medium, and hard test per chapter and moving on if accuracy was sufficient.
V: Covered key CR topics such as assumptions, strengthen/weaken, and paradox during the first study phase.
Maintained strong Verbal performance while Quant and DI caught up.
Used TTP's structured sequence rather than jumping randomly between topics.
DI: Did not study DI separately early on, but DI improved as Quant and Verbal foundations improved.
Used full-length custom tests near the end to practice pacing across all sections, including DI.
Learned from the score report that raw number of DI misses does not map directly to score because question difficulty matters.
overall: Improved from 525 to 675 during an intensive between-jobs study window.
Studied roughly 260 hours, often 7-9 hours per weekday while taking weekends off.
Followed the prep plan first, then adjusted based on mock review once enough data existed.
Used custom full-length tests and official mocks for pacing before the exam.
Prepared for at-home test logistics by downloading the secure browser early, connecting early, and staying calm during proctor checks.

V: Stabilized an inconsistent Verbal score by treating the section as a system with repeatable logic rather than a subjective coin flip.
Stopped eliminating choices too quickly based on instinct.
Learned not to reject correct answers merely because the wording sounded awkward or less polished.
Stayed engaged through dense RC passages rather than rereading mechanically.
For CR, located the conclusion before touching the answer choices.
Used mental-performance tools to stay deliberate under time pressure and prevent one hard question from disrupting the next several.
overall: Moved from a 585 starting point to 695, with Verbal becoming more predictable.
Worked with a tutor to target specific weak points rather than broadly studying more.
Identified score variability as both a skill issue and an execution-under-pressure issue.
Finished the process feeling the official result reflected execution rather than luck.
